extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces **real-time data exchange capabilities** to ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, implying sub-second to millisecond-level latency for critical operational monitoring and control. The framework supports dynamic operational needs, suggesting adaptive time scales for different services (e.g., high-frequency sensor data for building control vs. lower-frequency analytics for facility management). Temporal granularity is implicitly tied to the **runtime configuration management** feature, allowing adjustments to synchronization frequencies without full redeployment, though exact latency thresholds or granularity specifications are not explicitly quantified in the provided documents. The system bridges static BIM representations with dynamic operational requirements, implying a hybrid time-scale model where historical (BIM) and real-time (DT) data coexist for life-cycle management.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin framework supports a **centralized architecture** with a **modular, extensible design**, enabling multiple digital twin instances to coexist under a unified framework. **Hierarchical structure** is implied through role-based interactions: *Building Control Engineers* deploy device models (likely per-building or subsystem twins), *Facility Managers* interact with deployed DTs for real-time monitoring (potentially aggregated or federated twins), and *DT Engineers* extend capabilities via service integration (modular, plug-and-play components). Coordination mechanisms are facilitated by the **DT Framework** acting as a central hub for bidirectional data exchange, though decentralized coordination (e.g., peer-to-peer or edge-based twins) is not explicitly described. Scope of responsibilities is divided by roles: engineers handle deployment/extension, managers handle operations, and the framework orchestrates inter-instance synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system employs a **coordinated, role-based architecture pattern** where system components are organized into a **life-cycle pipeline** (enriched BIM models → operational DTs). Orchestration is achieved through **standardized extension points** in the modular framework, enabling dynamic integration of custom services and analytics. Resource management is implicit via **runtime configuration management**, allowing adjustments without redeployment, while system-wide coordination is centralized through the **DT Framework hub**, which facilitates bidirectional data exchange. The constellation leverages **interactive visualization** for immersive monitoring/control and **non-functional requirements (NF_X)** to ensure scalability, interoperability, and maintainability across heterogeneous components.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin framework prioritizes **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** as a non-functional requirement (NF_X), ensuring seamless integration with external systems via **standardized extension points** and **modular architecture**. Data exchange protocols are not explicitly specified, but the bidirectional synchronization between physical buildings and digital counterparts implies adherence to industry standards (e.g., BIM collaboration formats like IFC or COBie for static data, and OPC UA/MTConnect for real-time sensor/device data). Dependencies on external systems are managed through the **DT Framework’s central hub**, which acts as a mediator for enterprise systems (e.g., ERP, CMMS) or other Digital Twins. Integration patterns include **service-oriented architectures (SOA)** for analytics and **runtime-configurable plugins** for third-party tools, though exact protocols or APIs are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system employs a **model-driven deployment framework** leveraging **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to address the limitations of **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** in representing dynamic Building Control Systems (BCS) components. The framework supports **geometric models** (inherited from BIM) and **behavioral models** (enriched with device-specific configurations such as sensors, actuators, and controllers) to create a **unified semantic foundation**. Data components include **structured representations** extracted from BIM models via automated transformation processes, enabling the generation of **deployable DT artifacts** (e.g., runtime models, control logic, and service interfaces). The framework ensures **real-time data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, facilitated by **continuous synchronization mechanisms**. Data management approaches are modular, with **standardized extension points** for integrating custom services, analytics, and domain-specific models. Model relationships are established through **semantic mappings** between BIM elements (e.g., spatial hierarchies, component dependencies) and their DT counterparts, while **interactive visualization** interfaces provide immersive monitoring and control. The framework’s architecture supports **life-cycle management**, from initial device modeling to operational evolution, aligning with use cases for **Building Control Engineers**, **Facility Managers**, and **DT Engineers** (as defined in Table I).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin framework addresses **fidelity requirements** by enabling **Building Control Engineers** to define **sensors, actuators, and controllers** directly within the BIM environment, ensuring semantic alignment between physical and digital representations. **Validation and verification** are implicitly supported through the **automated generation of deployable DT artifacts** from enriched BIM models, minimizing manual intervention and reducing human-induced errors. **Uncertainty sources** are mitigated via **real-time data exchange** mechanisms that synchronize physical and digital states, though explicit quantification methods (e.g., probabilistic models or error bounds) are not detailed. **Error detection and correction** are facilitated by the framework’s **modular architecture**, allowing custom services to integrate **diagnostic analytics** or **anomaly detection** modules. **Quality assurance** processes are embedded in the **life-cycle deployment framework**, ensuring consistency across stages from device modeling to operational monitoring. The framework’s reliance on **MDE principles** and **standardized extension points** further supports **interoperability** and **reproducibility**, though specific fidelity metrics (e.g., temporal resolution, spatial accuracy) are not explicitly quantified in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin (DT) system is characterized by **real-time bidirectional data exchange** between the physical building infrastructure and its digital counterpart. Data flows include **sensor readings, operational metrics, environmental parameters (e.g., temperature, occupancy, energy consumption), and device status updates** from IoT-enabled building systems (e.g., HVAC, lighting, security). The framework supports **standardized data formats** (likely aligned with BIM tools like IFC or COBie) for seamless integration with enriched BIM models. **Communication protocols** are implied to be **low-latency, high-reliability** (e.g., MQTT, OPC UA, or proprietary protocols) to ensure continuous synchronization. **Transmission frequencies** are not explicitly quantified but are inferred to be **event-driven or periodic** based on operational needs (e.g., critical alerts vs. routine monitoring). **Data quality assurance** is addressed through the framework’s **runtime configuration management**, enabling dynamic adjustments to data validation rules without redeployment. **Preprocessing steps** include **enrichment of raw BIM models** (e.g., adding semantic metadata, device hierarchies) to bridge static representations with dynamic operational data. **Data integration mechanisms** leverage the **modular architecture** with standardized extension points, allowing custom analytics or middleware services to process and normalize incoming data streams before ingestion into the DT core. The system emphasizes **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** (e.g., legacy building automation systems and modern IoT platforms) as a non-functional requirement (NF_X).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this DT system enables **real-time control commands** from the digital twin to physical building systems, executed by **Facility Managers** via the deployed DT interface. **Command types** include **adjustments to HVAC setpoints, lighting schedules, security protocols, and energy optimization directives**, derived from analytics or manual overrides. **Control message formats** are standardized to ensure compatibility with building automation systems (e.g., BACnet, LonWorks, or proprietary protocols). **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are implied to be part of the **runtime configuration management**, ensuring only authorized commands (e.g., from authenticated Facility Managers or DT Engineers) are executed. **Feedback systems** provide **closed-loop confirmation** (e.g., acknowledgment signals from physical devices) to validate command execution, while **conflict resolution** is handled via the **centralized DT Framework**, which arbitrates competing commands (e.g., prioritizing safety overrides over energy-saving directives). **Command execution confirmation** is achieved through **bidirectional data exchange**, where the physical system’s response (e.g., sensor feedback post-adjustment) is looped back to the DT for validation. The system’s **modular architecture** allows for **custom service integration** to extend control logic (e.g., AI-driven predictive maintenance triggers).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for this DT system is designed to support **heterogeneous, interoperable communication** across building systems, IoT devices, and cloud/edge platforms. **Communication protocols** are not explicitly named but must include **industry-standard options** (e.g., OPC UA for machine-to-machine, MQTT for lightweight IoT, or HTTP/REST for web services) to ensure **scalability and interoperability**. The **network topology** is inferred to be a **hybrid architecture**, combining **edge nodes** (for low-latency local processing) and **central cloud hubs** (for global analytics and storage). **Bandwidth requirements** are implied to be **high** to accommodate real-time data streams from dense sensor networks, with **latency constraints** prioritizing **sub-second response times** for critical control loops (e.g., safety systems). **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are addressed through the framework’s **non-functional requirements (NF_X)**, emphasizing **fault tolerance** (e.g., failover protocols, data replication) and **accessibility** for diverse users. **Security protocols** include **authentication/authorization** (e.g., role-based access control for Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers) and **data encryption** (e.g., TLS for network traffic) to protect against unauthorized access or tampering. The system’s **modular architecture** with standardized extension points allows for **plug-and-play integration** of third-party network services (e.g., cybersecurity middleware).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **deployment infrastructure** for this DT system supports **multi-tier hosting models**, including **cloud, edge, and on-premise** options, tailored to the specific needs of the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) domain. **Computational requirements** are dynamic, scaling from **lightweight edge processing** (for real-time local analytics) to **high-performance cloud clusters** (for large-scale simulations or historical data analysis). **Storage requirements** are optimized for **high-velocity data ingestion** (e.g., time-series databases for sensor logs) and **long-term archival** (e.g., BIM model versions, operational logs). **Scalability mechanisms** are enabled by the **modular architecture**, allowing **horizontal scaling** of components (e.g., adding more analytics services or data nodes) without full redeployment, as supported by **runtime configuration management**. **Deployment models** include **hybrid cloud-edge setups**, where **BIM-enriched models** are initially processed on-premise by **Building Control Engineers**, while **operational DTs** are deployed in cloud or edge environments for **Facility Managers** to interact with. **Availability and reliability assurance** are addressed through the framework’s **non-functional requirements (NF_X)**, ensuring **high uptime** (e.g., via redundant hosting zones) and **low-overhead maintainability** (e.g., automated updates, minimal downtime for patches). The **DT Framework** acts as a **central hub**, abstracting underlying infrastructure complexities to provide a unified interface for all stakeholders.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the proposed model-driven deployment framework spans from **initial device modeling** to **custom DT service development**, addressing the gap between static BIM representations and dynamic DT requirements. Key phases include:
  1. **Device Modeling & Configuration**: Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators, and controllers within the BIM environment (e.g., via **F1** in Table III), creating a unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformations.
  2. **Model Transformation & Artifact Generation**: Leveraging **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles, the framework extracts structured representations from enriched BIM models and **automatically generates deployable DT artifacts** with minimal manual intervention.
  3. **Deployment & Synchronization**: Real-time data exchange ensures continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, enabling dynamic updates.
  4. **Operation & Monitoring**: Facility Managers interact with the DT for **monitoring system states** and **operating physical infrastructure**, while DT Engineers extend functionality via custom services.
  5. **Evolution & Extension**: The modular architecture supports **runtime integration of custom services and analytics**, allowing the DT to adapt to changing operational requirements (e.g., via standardized extension points). Governance is implicit through **stakeholder-specific roles** (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and **use-case-driven requirements** (Table II).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process in this framework is **model-driven**, relying on **MDE principles** to bridge the gap between static BIM models and dynamic DTs. Key aspects include:
  - **Methodologies**: The framework employs **automated model transformations** to extract structured representations from BIM, enabling seamless generation of deployable DT artifacts (e.g., sensors, actuators, controllers). This is underpinned by **functional requirements (F_X)** (Table III), which ensure device modeling capabilities.
  - **Quality Assurance & Testing**: While not explicitly detailed, the **automated generation of artifacts** implies validation through **structured BIM-to-DT transformations**, reducing manual errors. Real-time synchronization mechanisms ensure **data consistency** between physical and digital systems.
  - **Version Control**: Not explicitly mentioned, but the **modular architecture** and **standardized extension points** suggest support for incremental updates and versioning of custom services/plugins.
  - **Evolution Management**: The DT evolves through **runtime integration of custom services** (developed by DT Engineers) and **interactive visualization interfaces**, allowing dynamic adaptation to operational needs. The framework’s **modularity** ensures backward compatibility while enabling extensions (e.g., analytics, control logic).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The framework supports **decision-making through immersive monitoring and control interfaces**, leveraging:
  - **Analytics & Processing**: Custom services and plugins (integrated via standardized extension points) enable **real-time analytics** and **operational insights**, though specific algorithms or tools are not detailed.
  - **User Interaction**: Facility Managers interact with the DT via **interactive visualization**, allowing them to monitor system states and operate physical infrastructure. Building Control Engineers define **control logic** (e.g., sensor/actuator configurations) within BIM, while DT Engineers extend functionality.
  - **Decision Support**: Insights are generated through **real-time data exchange** between physical and digital systems, enabling **dynamic decision-making** (e.g., fault detection, optimization). Confidence measures are implied by **automated synchronization** and **structured model transformations**, though explicit metrics (e.g., accuracy thresholds) are not provided.
  - **Types of Insights**: Likely include **system state monitoring**, **predictive maintenance alerts**, and **control logic validation**, derived from synchronized BIM-DT data.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.
block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS
block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
